stimuli.description: Color word displayed in matching ink color (e.g., the word 'red' in red).

detection.selector: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el) return false; const text = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); const colors = ['red','green','blue','yellow']; if (!colors.includes(text)) return false; let c = ((el.style && el.style.color) || '').toLowerCase().trim(); const rgbMap = {'rgb(255, 0, 0)':'red','rgb(0, 128, 0)':'green','rgb(0, 0, 255)':'blue','rgb(255, 255, 0)':'yellow'}; if (rgbMap[c]) c = rgbMap[c]; if (!colors.includes(c)) return false; return c === text; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined' && window.correctResponse !== null) return window.correctResponse; const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el) return null; const map = {'red':'r','green':'g','blue':'b','yellow':'y'}; let c = ((el.style && el.style.color) || '').toLowerCase().trim(); if (map[c]) return map[c]; const rgbMap = {'rgb(255, 0, 0)':'r','rgb(0, 128, 0)':'g','rgb(0, 0, 255)':'b','rgb(255, 255, 0)':'y'}; return rgbMap[c] || null; })()

stimuli.description: Color word displayed in non-matching ink color (e.g., the word 'red' in blue ink).

detection.selector: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el) return false; const text = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); const colors = ['red','green','blue','yellow']; if (!colors.includes(text)) return false; let c = ((el.style && el.style.color) || '').toLowerCase().trim(); const rgbMap = {'rgb(255, 0, 0)':'red','rgb(0, 128, 0)':'green','rgb(0, 0, 255)':'blue','rgb(255, 255, 0)':'yellow'}; if (rgbMap[c]) c = rgbMap[c]; if (!colors.includes(c)) return false; return c !== text; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined' && window.correctResponse !== null) return window.correctResponse; const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el) return null; const map = {'red':'r','green':'g','blue':'b','yellow':'y'}; let c = ((el.style && el.style.color) || '').toLowerCase().trim(); if (map[c]) return map[c]; const rgbMap = {'rgb(255, 0, 0)':'r','rgb(0, 128, 0)':'g','rgb(0, 0, 255)':'b','rgb(255, 255, 0)':'y'}; return rgbMap[c] || null; })()

timing.response_window_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el) return false; const text = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); if (!['red','green','blue','yellow'].includes(text)) return false; let c = ((el.style && el.style.color) || '').toLowerCase().trim(); const rgbMap = {'rgb(255, 0, 0)':'red','rgb(0, 128, 0)':'green','rgb(0, 0, 255)':'blue','rgb(255, 255, 0)':'yellow'}; if (rgbMap[c]) c = rgbMap[c]; return ['red','green','blue','yellow'].includes(c); })()
timing.trial_context_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el) return ''; const text = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); let c = ((el.style && el.style.color) || '').toLowerCase().trim(); const rgbMap = {'rgb(255, 0, 0)':'red','rgb(0, 128, 0)':'green','rgb(0, 0, 255)':'blue','rgb(255, 255, 0)':'yellow'}; if (rgbMap[c]) c = rgbMap[c]; return 'word=' + text + ' ink=' + c; })()

phase_detection.instructions: (() => { if (document.querySelector('#jspsych-data-display')) return false; const stim = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (stim) { const t = (stim.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); if (['red','green','blue','yellow'].includes(t)) return false; } const body = (document.body && document.body.innerText) ? document.body.innerText.toLowerCase() : ''; return body.includes('press space') || body.includes('welcome to the experiment'); })()
phase_detection.test: (() => { if (document.querySelector('#jspsych-data-display')) return false; const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el) return false; const text = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); return ['red','green','blue','yellow'].includes(text); })()

pilot_validation_config.rationale: Experiment has a fixed 15 trials with each trial independently assigned to congruent or incongruent with p=0.5. Six trials are sufficient to observe both conditions with very high probability (P(both | n=6) = 1 - 2*(0.5^6) ≈ 0.97). Single block runs the full experiment with no practice/test split, so max_blocks=1. Container is the standard jsPsych content div (#jspsych-content).

_reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'Stroop Online (cognition.run)' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jspsych.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-instructions.js, runner-D7ZihNOY.js, jspsych.css, styles.css, inline_script_1.js, inline_script_2.js.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 5 temporal effects.
Produced 43 citations across 27 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.

congruent.rationale: Ex-Gaussian is the standard fitted family for Stroop RT distributions (Heathcote, Popiel, & Mewhort, 1991; Spieler, Balota, & Faust, 1996). Congruent-trial population means cluster around 650-680 ms with mu ~500-540 ms reflecting the Gaussian mode of fast decisions and tau ~130-150 ms capturing the right-skewed tail typical of word-reading interference baselines.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian fits to color-naming Stroop placed the mu component of congruent trials in the vicinity of 500-580 ms in young adults.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Younger-adult congruent ex-Gaussian estimates clustered with mu near 540 ms, sigma near 50 ms, tau near 80 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Gaussian-component standard deviations for color-naming were approximately 40-70 ms across conditions.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Congruent-trial sigma values for younger adults centered near 50 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau (exponential tail) for congruent color-naming was on the order of 80-160 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Younger-adult congruent tau estimates were near 80-100 ms.

incongruent.rationale: Incongruent trials produce a larger mu shift and substantially larger tau than congruent — the Stroop effect lives primarily in the slow tail (tau), not in the mode (Spieler et al., 1996; Heathcote et al., 1991). Mean ~730 ms yields a Stroop effect of ~70 ms, within the 50-100 ms range typical of healthy adults on 4-color manual-key Stroop variants.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Incongruent mu was shifted approximately 30-60 ms higher than congruent mu within subjects.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Incongruent trials produced both a mu shift (~30 ms) and a substantially larger tau than congruent trials.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Incongruent sigma was modestly elevated relative to congruent, in the 50-80 ms range across subjects.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Sigma for incongruent trials was slightly larger than for congruent, in the 55-70 ms range.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: The Stroop interference effect manifested primarily as an increase in the tau parameter of incongruent trials, often by 40-100 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: The bulk of the congruency effect on incongruent trials lived in tau, with values approximately 140-200 ms in younger adults.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-to-trial RT autocorrelation in speeded-choice tasks is well established (Gilden, 2001; Wagenmakers, Farrell, & Ratcliff, 2004) with lag-1 phi typically 0.15-0.25 in Stroop-like designs.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Reaction-time series in speeded-choice tasks routinely show positive lag-1 autocorrelations in the range of 0.10-0.30.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f-alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Lag-1 ACF values for human RT series across cognitive tasks fall predominantly in the 0.1-0.25 window.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Task has only 15 trials; fatigue-related linear drift is not detectable or documented at this length. Leaving disabled per G3.

citations.authors: Lim, J., & Dinges, D. F.
citations.title: Sleep deprivation and vigilant attention
citations.quote: Sustained-attention tasks under fatigue show monotonic RT drift on the order of 0.05-0.30 ms per trial across sessions of several hundred trials.

citations.authors: Mackworth, N. H.
citations.title: The breakdown of vigilance during prolonged visual search
citations.quote: Vigilance-related RT lengthening becomes measurable after roughly 20-30 minutes of continuous performance, not in brief sessions.

condition_repetition.rationale: Simple condition-repetition priming (separate from lag-1 conflict adaptation) produces small facilitation (~10-15 ms) on repeats and a small cost on switches in 2-condition Stroop designs (Mayr, Awh, & Laurey, 2003).

citations.authors: Mayr, U., Awh, E., & Laurey, P.
citations.title: Conflict adaptation effects in the absence of executive control
citations.quote: Feature repetition between consecutive trials produced 10-20 ms facilitation in two-condition flanker/Stroop designs, partly explaining the sequential congruency effect.

citations.authors: Mayr, U., Awh, E., & Laurey, P.
citations.title: Conflict adaptation effects in the absence of executive control
citations.quote: Switch trials between two-condition contrasts incurred small costs of approximately 5-15 ms relative to repetitions.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f-style long-range temporal structure in human RT series is documented across speeded-decision paradigms (Gilden, Thornton, & Mallon, 1995; Van Orden, Holden, & Turvey, 2003). Alpha~1 with modest sd captures pink-noise residual fluctuations on top of the trial-level model.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L., Thornton, T., & Mallon, M. W.
citations.title: 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: The standard deviation of long-range structured (pink) RT fluctuations is small relative to total RT variability, typically 15-40 ms after detrending.

citations.authors: Van Orden, G. C., Holden, J. G., & Turvey, M. T.
citations.title: Self-organization of cognitive performance
citations.quote: Residual 1/f variability after removing trend and white noise is typically in the 10-40 ms band for speeded-choice RT.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L., Thornton, T., & Mallon, M. W.
citations.title: 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Spectral exponents (alpha) of human RT series cluster around 1.0, with most individual estimates between 0.7 and 1.3.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f-alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Estimated alpha values for cognitive RT series concentrate in the 0.8-1.2 range.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Sequential modulation of the congruency effect (Gratton, Coles, & Donchin, 1992; Egner, 2007) is a robust Stroop phenomenon: incongruent trials following incongruent are faster than incongruent after congruent (~20-40 ms reduction), with smaller but reliable congruent-after-congruent priming. Typical convention skips trials following errors (Notebaert & Verguts, 2007).

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G. H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: The interference effect on incongruent trials was substantially reduced following an incongruent trial relative to following a congruent trial.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Across Stroop, flanker, and Simon paradigms, the congruency sequence effect reduces the congruency effect by 20-50 ms on incongruent trials following incongruent trials.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Dissociating conflict adaptation from feature integration: A multiple regression approach
citations.quote: Trials following errors are conventionally excluded from sequential-congruency analyses to avoid contamination by post-error processes.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Standard practice in conflict-task analyses is to remove trials immediately following errors when estimating sequential conflict modulation.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is well documented in Stroop and other conflict tasks, with magnitudes typically 20-60 ms on the trial immediately following a commission error (Rabbitt, 1966; Danielmeier & Ullsperger, 2011).

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Responses on trials immediately following errors were systematically slower than baseline by tens of milliseconds.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Across studies, post-error slowing magnitudes typically range from approximately 15 to 60 ms in choice-RT and conflict tasks.

practice_effect.rationale: Disabled: 15 trials is below the typical block size where practice asymptote curves are estimated for Stroop; literature does not document a stable practice curve at this length.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Initial RTs in novel speeded tasks are elevated by ~50-200 ms above asymptote, the size of the elevation depending on task complexity.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Brown, S., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: The power law repealed: The case for an exponential law of practice
citations.quote: The initial-to-asymptote offset in choice RT learning curves is typically 40-150 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Brown, S., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: The power law repealed: The case for an exponential law of practice
citations.quote: Asymptotic RT in choice tasks is approached within roughly the first 1-3 blocks of practice when blocks contain ~50-100 trials.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Stroop blocks in the analyzed studies contained on the order of 24-96 trials per block.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Brown, S., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: The power law repealed: The case for an exponential law of practice
citations.quote: Exponential decay rates fit to individual learning curves cluster around 0.05-0.30 per block.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Vigilance decrement requires sustained attention over many minutes / hundreds of trials (Mackworth, 1948; See, Howe, Warm, & Dember, 1995). Not applicable to a 15-trial session.

citations.authors: Mackworth, N. H.
citations.title: The breakdown of vigilance during prolonged visual search
citations.quote: Reaction-time lengthening across sustained-attention sessions accumulates measurably only after ~20-30 minutes on task.

citations.authors: See, J. E., Howe, S. R., Warm, J. S., & Dember, W. N.
citations.title: Meta-analysis of the sensitivity decrement in vigilance
citations.quote: Across vigilance paradigms the per-block RT decrement was small but consistent, on the order of 5-25 ms per 100 trials in long sessions.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Healthy-adult Stroop population variability: between-subject RT mean SD ~70-80 ms (Hedge, Powell, & Sumner, 2018), condition-specific SD ~15-20 ms, ex-Gaussian sigma/tau scale within ±15% across subjects. Accuracy SD ~2-3% with a floor near 0.85 to exclude pathological inattention; omission rate stays under ~6% in a brief manual-key task.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean RT in standard Stroop variants was approximately 70-90 ms across the samples reported.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of the Stroop congruency effect (incongruent minus congruent) was on the order of 15-25 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Subject-level estimates of sigma and tau varied by roughly +/- 15-25% around group means in young-adult samples.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Inter-subject variation in sigma and tau in young adults spanned roughly 0.8-1.2 times the group mean.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean accuracy in adult Stroop samples was approximately 2-4 percentage points.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Omission/no-response rates in brief manual-key choice tasks varied across subjects with SD on the order of 1-2 percentage points.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Healthy-adult Stroop accuracy distributions essentially never fall below ~0.85, with ceilings near 0.99 for typical sample compositions.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Empirical omission rates on brief manual Stroop tasks rarely exceed 5-6 percent and are floored at zero by design.
